chunks.title: Every_Python_Function_Explained___H_-_Z_online-video-cutter_WMi3hwO.com
chunks.text: function and get added into a new map object. And then what we can do is convert that map map object

chunks.title: Every_Python_Function_Explained___H_-_Z_online-video-cutter_WMi3hwO.com
chunks.text: to a list story. And now we'll get the squares of every single element from our sequence. Now you

chunks.title: Every_Python_Function_Explained___H_-_Z_online-video-cutter_WMi3hwO.com
chunks.text: can do the same thing here with a dictionary. So I've created kind of a more advanced example,

chunks.title: Every_Python_Function_Explained___H_-_Z_online-video-cutter_WMi3hwO.com
chunks.text: I guess we have a function. What this function does is it takes items, which is this array or

chunks.title: Every_Python_Function_Explained___H_-_Z_online-video-cutter_WMi3hwO.com
chunks.text: this list at whatever key is passed to the function. And then it multiplies it by the key.

chunks.title: Every_Python_Function_Explained___H_-_Z_online-video-cutter_WMi3hwO.com
chunks.text: Now, in this case, we're going to have string multiplication and you'll see what kind of

chunks.title: Every_Python_Function_Explained___H_-_Z_online-video-cutter_WMi3hwO.com
chunks.text: happens in this example. So if I run this, we get all of the squares from one to 10,

chunks.title: Every_Python_Function_Explained___H_-_Z_online-video-cutter_WMi3hwO.com
chunks.text: and then we get a BBB and then four C's. Now, the reason we get that result down here

chunks.title: Every_Python_Function_Explained___H_-_Z_online-video-cutter_WMi3hwO.com
chunks.text: is because when you iterate over a dictionary, you're going to get every single key. That's

chunks.title: Every_Python_Function_Explained___H_-_Z_online-video-cutter_WMi3hwO.com
chunks.text: what's going to be passed to your map function. So we pass the key, we get the value associated

chunks.title: Every_Python_Function_Explained___H_-_Z_online-video-cutter_WMi3hwO.com
chunks.text: with the key. And then we multiply that by the key. Since the key is a string, that's then going

chunks.title: Every_Python_Function_Explained___H_-_Z_online-video-cutter_WMi3hwO.com
chunks.text: to give a string multiplication. And that's why we get four C's, three B's and one A moving on.

chunks.title: Every_Python_Function_Explained___H_-_Z_online-video-cutter_WMi3hwO.com
chunks.text: We have the max function. This allows us to get the maximum value out of an iterable sequence or

chunks.title: Every_Python_Function_Explained___H_-_Z_online-video-cutter_WMi3hwO.com
chunks.text: of multiple arguments. There's different ways you can call this. First of all, we can pass like a

chunks.title: Every_Python_Function_Explained___H_-_Z_online-video-cutter_WMi3hwO.com
chunks.text: list and it's just going to give us the maximum value in this list. We can also pass numbers or

chunks.title: Every_Python_Function_Explained___H_-_Z_online-video-cutter_WMi3hwO.com
chunks.text: arguments individually. So I've done that here. We can do the same with strings. Now, when we're

chunks.title: Every_Python_Function_Explained___H_-_Z_online-video-cutter_WMi3hwO.com
chunks.text: comparing strings, we're going to be looking at the ASCII value of each character starting at the

chunks.title: Every_Python_Function_Explained___H_-_Z_online-video-cutter_WMi3hwO.com
chunks.text: first character. So we'd start by comparing each string using the first character. If two strings

chunks.title: Every_Python_Function_Explained___H_-_Z_online-video-cutter_WMi3hwO.com
chunks.text: have the same first character, then we will look at the second character and use the ASCII value of

chunks.title: Every_Python_Function_Explained___H_-_Z_online-video-cutter_WMi3hwO.com
chunks.text: that character. Next, we have just passing multiple iterable sequences here. Now, whenever you pass

chunks.title: Every_Python_Function_Explained___H_-_Z_online-video-cutter_WMi3hwO.com
chunks.text: multiple values, so like multiple parameters to this function, they need to be the same type.

chunks.title: Every_Python_Function_Explained___H_-_Z_online-video-cutter_WMi3hwO.com
chunks.text: So in this case, I'm passing three lists. And what it's going to do to compare the list is actually

chunks.title: Every_Python_Function_Explained___H_-_Z_online-video-cutter_WMi3hwO.com
chunks.text: use the first element of these lists. So we'll have this being the maximum element because this

chunks.title: Every_Python_Function_Explained___H_-_Z_online-video-cutter_WMi3hwO.com
chunks.text: list has the greatest first element in it. So just like comparing the strings, it works the same for

chunks.title: Every_Python_Function_Explained___H_-_Z_online-video-cutter_WMi3hwO.com
chunks.text: a list. We can also use this on like a custom type. So I have a custom type here and I can pass

chunks.title: Every_Python_Function_Explained___H_-_Z_online-video-cutter_WMi3hwO.com
chunks.text: the key function or the key parameter, sorry, to my map function. And now we're going to be using

chunks.title: Every_Python_Function_Explained___H_-_Z_online-video-cutter_WMi3hwO.com
chunks.text: this to compare. So we'll pass every single value to this key function. And then whatever the result

chunks.title: Every_Python_Function_Explained___H_-_Z_online-video-cutter_WMi3hwO.com
chunks.text: of that key function is

chunks.title: Every_Python_Function_Explained___H_-_Z_online-video-cutter_WMi3hwO.com
chunks.text: we'll use to pick the

chunks.title: Every_Python_Function_Explained___H_-_Z_online-video-cutter_WMi3hwO.com
chunks.text: largest value. So let me

chunks.title: Every_Python_Function_Explained___H_-_Z_online-video-cutter_WMi3hwO.com
chunks.text: run this and notice we

chunks.title: Every_Python_Function_Explained___H_-_Z_online-video-cutter_WMi3hwO.com
chunks.text: get 34 nine. Yes, we

chunks.title: Every_Python_Function_Explained___H_-_Z_online-video-cutter_WMi3hwO.com
chunks.text: get to one, as I said,

chunks.title: Every_Python_Function_Explained___H_-_Z_online-video-cutter_WMi3hwO.com
chunks.text: and then we get a

chunks.title: Every_Python_Function_Explained___H_-_Z_online-video-cutter_WMi3hwO.com
chunks.text: custom object If I were to print that out you would see that we would get C three uh because the value returned by C three is the largest All right So the next function is a memory view

chunks.title: Every_Python_Function_Explained___H_-_Z_online-video-cutter_WMi3hwO.com
chunks.text: This is a little bit complicated, but you can use this on bytes types and a few other types as well

chunks.title: Every_Python_Function_Explained___H_-_Z_online-video-cutter_WMi3hwO.com
chunks.text: here in Python to kind of dig into the representation of this object. So I have a memory

chunks.title: Every_Python_Function_Explained___H_-_Z_online-video-cutter_WMi3hwO.com
chunks.text: view of my variable X, which is a bytes type. That's what the B stands for here. And I can look

chunks.title: Every_Python_Function_Explained___H_-_Z_online-video-cutter_WMi3hwO.com
chunks.text: at, for example, the first index, which would correspond with B. Now notice that when I print

chunks.title: Every_Python_Function_Explained___H_-_Z_online-video-cutter_WMi3hwO.com
chunks.text: this out, the first value I get is 98. So what's actually stored in memory for the character B is

chunks.title: Every_Python_Function_Explained___H_-_Z_online-video-cutter_WMi3hwO.com
chunks.text: 98 and 98 is the ASCII representation of this character. So if I then convert that ASCII

chunks.title: Every_Python_Function_Explained___H_-_Z_online-video-cutter_WMi3hwO.com
chunks.text: representation to a character, notice I get B. And so I'm looking at this character here at index

chunks.title: Every_Python_Function_Explained___H_-_Z_online-video-cutter_WMi3hwO.com
chunks.text: one or position one. If I try to print out a slice of the memory, I can do this and I'm going to get

chunks.title: Every_Python_Function_Explained___H_-_Z_online-video-cutter_WMi3hwO.com
chunks.text: a memory view object, or what I can do is convert this slice to a bytes type. And now I'm going to

chunks.title: Every_Python_Function_Explained___H_-_Z_online-video-cutter_WMi3hwO.com
chunks.text: get BCD, right? So if I'm going from one to four, that would be here. So I'm getting BCD when I

chunks.title: Every_Python_Function_Explained___H_-_Z_online-video-cutter_WMi3hwO.com
chunks.text: actually convert that to bytes. If I don't convert it to bytes, then of course I get the memory

chunks.title: Every_Python_Function_Explained___H_-_Z_online-video-cutter_WMi3hwO.com
chunks.text: representation of that bytes object. All right, moving on, we have the min function. Now this is

chunks.title: Every_Python_Function_Explained___H_-_Z_online-video-cutter_WMi3hwO.com
chunks.text: identical to the max function, just the minimum value. I don't really need to go through this

chunks.title: Every_Python_Function_Explained___H_-_Z_online-video-cutter_WMi3hwO.com
chunks.text: Note that you can use the keyword here as well and no pun intended, but you can use key as a keyword.

chunks.title: Every_Python_Function_Explained___H_-_Z_online-video-cutter_WMi3hwO.com
chunks.text: And then that allows you to select the minimum based on the return value of this function.

chunks.title: Every_Python_Function_Explained___H_-_Z_online-video-cutter_WMi3hwO.com
chunks.text: Now, next, we're talking about the next function.

chunks.title: Every_Python_Function_Explained___H_-_Z_online-video-cutter_WMi3hwO.com
chunks.text: Now, this is similar to the next function that we saw in part one, except this is for regular iterators, not asynchronous iterators.

chunks.title: Every_Python_Function_Explained___H_-_Z_online-video-cutter_WMi3hwO.com
chunks.text: What this will do is give you the next value in an iterable sequence, assuming that whatever you're calling this on is an iterator.

chunks.title: Every_Python_Function_Explained___H_-_Z_online-video-cutter_WMi3hwO.com
chunks.text: So I have my custom iterator object here.

chunks.title: Every_Python_Function_Explained___H_-_Z_online-video-cutter_WMi3hwO.com
chunks.text: notice that I implement my inner method as well as my next method. And now what I can do is create

chunks.title: Every_Python_Function_Explained___H_-_Z_online-video-cutter_WMi3hwO.com
chunks.text: an inner or an iterator, sorry, from my custom object. So I create an iterator and then I can

chunks.title: Every_Python_Function_Explained___H_-_Z_online-video-cutter_WMi3hwO.com
chunks.text: call next on it and I will get the next value in the interval sequence. And I can do the same thing

chunks.title: Every_Python_Function_Explained___H_-_Z_online-video-cutter_WMi3hwO.com
chunks.text: using, um, like a built-in object, right? So if I have a list, I can actually get an iterator for

chunks.title: Every_Python_Function_Explained___H_-_Z_online-video-cutter_WMi3hwO.com
chunks.text: this list. And then I can call next on that list iterator and get the next value. So let me show

chunks.title: Every_Python_Function_Explained___H_-_Z_online-video-cutter_WMi3hwO.com
chunks.text: this with one, two, three, a B C again, very similar to the a next function, just for regular

chunks.title: Every_Python_Function_Explained___H_-_Z_online-video-cutter_WMi3hwO.com
chunks.text: iterators, not asynchronous iterators. All right. So moving on, we have the object function.

chunks.title: Every_Python_Function_Explained___H_-_Z_online-video-cutter_WMi3hwO.com
chunks.text: This is going to create an empty object for you. If you didn't know every single class in Python

chunks.title: Every_Python_Function_Explained___H_-_Z_online-video-cutter_WMi3hwO.com
chunks.text: by default inherits from object So it has some prebuilt functionality It not super useful but I will run the code and show you that we can print out an object of type object and we can view all of the kind of

chunks.title: Every_Python_Function_Explained___H_-_Z_online-video-cutter_WMi3hwO.com
chunks.text: built-in behavior, default behavior for this type. Moving on, we have the oct function,

chunks.title: Every_Python_Function_Explained___H_-_Z_online-video-cutter_WMi3hwO.com
chunks.text: which stands for octal. It's going to give us the base eight representation of a base 10 number.

chunks.title: Every_Python_Function_Explained___H_-_Z_online-video-cutter_WMi3hwO.com
chunks.text: So if I print these out, you can see that that's what we're getting. Really, you want to be looking

chunks.title: Every_Python_Function_Explained___H_-_Z_online-video-cutter_WMi3hwO.com
chunks.text: at the part after the zero O and notice that you get a negative zero O whenever you have a negative

chunks.title: Every_Python_Function_Explained___H_-_Z_online-video-cutter_WMi3hwO.com
chunks.text: of octal value. Don't need to go through this too much more, but this gives you the octal value.

chunks.title: Every_Python_Function_Explained___H_-_Z_online-video-cutter_WMi3hwO.com
chunks.text: All right. The next function I have for you is open. Now what this does is open a new file. Now

chunks.title: Every_Python_Function_Explained___H_-_Z_online-video-cutter_WMi3hwO.com
chunks.text: I've put all of the different modes that you can use when opening a file here. I'm not going to go

chunks.title: Every_Python_Function_Explained___H_-_Z_online-video-cutter_WMi3hwO.com
chunks.text: through them. Feel free to pause the video and read them if you want, but let's look at this

chunks.title: Every_Python_Function_Explained___H_-_Z_online-video-cutter_WMi3hwO.com
chunks.text: example down here. So I import the Jason module just to show you how we can kind of save Jason

chunks.title: Every_Python_Function_Explained___H_-_Z_online-video-cutter_WMi3hwO.com
chunks.text: data. Anyways, what I can do is use this open object to open a file in read mode or in write

chunks.title: Every_Python_Function_Explained___H_-_Z_online-video-cutter_WMi3hwO.com
chunks.text: mode or whatever the other modes are. So you put the name of the file and then the mode you want to

chunks.title: Every_Python_Function_Explained___H_-_Z_online-video-cutter_WMi3hwO.com
chunks.text: open it in. In this case, lend up high as a file that does exist. Then if I wanted to read all of

chunks.title: Every_Python_Function_Explained___H_-_Z_online-video-cutter_WMi3hwO.com
chunks.text: the lines, I can use the file object returned by open to say F dot read lines. This will give me a

chunks.title: Every_Python_Function_Explained___H_-_Z_online-video-cutter_WMi3hwO.com
chunks.text: list of all of the lines that exist. I can print those out and then I can close the file object.

chunks.title: Every_Python_Function_Explained___H_-_Z_online-video-cutter_WMi3hwO.com
chunks.text: Now, the better approach to do this is to use this width, which really is going to give you a context

chunks.title: Every_Python_Function_Explained___H_-_Z_online-video-cutter_WMi3hwO.com
chunks.text: manager when you're kind of using this open function. So you say with open as F lines equals

chunks.title: Every_Python_Function_Explained___H_-_Z_online-video-cutter_WMi3hwO.com
chunks.text: F dot read lines and then print lines. And now you don't need to manually close the file

chunks.title: Every_Python_Function_Explained___H_-_Z_online-video-cutter_WMi3hwO.com
chunks.text: because as soon as you escape this with block, it's automatically going to close the file for you.

chunks.title: Every_Python_Function_Explained___H_-_Z_online-video-cutter_WMi3hwO.com
chunks.text: Continuing, I just wanted to show you how we can save JSON data. So I'm saying data is equal to,

chunks.title: Every_Python_Function_Explained___H_-_Z_online-video-cutter_WMi3hwO.com
chunks.text: and then some dictionary, then I'm going to open a new JSON file that doesn't already exist. So

chunks.title: Every_Python_Function_Explained___H_-_Z_online-video-cutter_WMi3hwO.com
chunks.text: data dot JSON in W mode, which stands for right mode. I open this as F and then I can say JSON

chunks.title: Every_Python_Function_Explained___H_-_Z_online-video-cutter_WMi3hwO.com
chunks.text: dot dump and then data, which is my dictionary and then into the file F and this will create a

chunks.title: Every_Python_Function_Explained___H_-_Z_online-video-cutter_WMi3hwO.com
chunks.text: file that has that content. So if I run this code here, you can see that I'm just printing out the

chunks.title: Every_Python_Function_Explained___H_-_Z_online-video-cutter_WMi3hwO.com
chunks.text: contents of the Lenda PI file. But now if we look here, I have this data dot JSON file that I created,

chunks.title: Every_Python_Function_Explained___H_-_Z_online-video-cutter_WMi3hwO.com
chunks.text: uh, using the open function. Moving on, we have the PAL function. Now what PAL will do is raise a

chunks.title: Every_Python_Function_Explained___H_-_Z_online-video-cutter_WMi3hwO.com
chunks.text: base to an exponent. We also have the option to get the modulus of a base raised to an exponent

chunks.title: Every_Python_Function_Explained___H_-_Z_online-video-cutter_WMi3hwO.com
chunks.text: if we want. So if we use the power, normally we just pass our base and our exponent and we can get

chunks.title: Every_Python_Function_Explained___H_-_Z_online-video-cutter_WMi3hwO.com
chunks.text: 100. And we can also get

chunks.title: Every_Python_Function_Explained___H_-_Z_online-video-cutter_WMi3hwO.com
chunks.text: 0.01 here. If we pass like a

chunks.title: Every_Python_Function_Explained___H_-_Z_online-video-cutter_WMi3hwO.com
chunks.text: negative two as the

chunks.title: Every_Python_Function_Explained___H_-_Z_online-video-cutter_WMi3hwO.com
chunks.text: exponent continuing, we have

chunks.title: Every_Python_Function_Explained___H_-_Z_online-video-cutter_WMi3hwO.com
chunks.text: power with mod. Now, if you

chunks.title: Every_Python_Function_Explained___H_-_Z_online-video-cutter_WMi3hwO.com
chunks.text: pass a third argument here

chunks.title: Every_Python_Function_Explained___H_-_Z_online-video-cutter_WMi3hwO.com
chunks.text: to the power function you going to raise the base to the exponent then modulus it by whatever this is So mod six And in this case you can see this is kind of the order of operations We raise 11 to the exponent three that gives us this value We then mod six that going to give us five

chunks.title: Every_Python_Function_Explained___H_-_Z_online-video-cutter_WMi3hwO.com
chunks.text: So when I print this out, I get a hundred zero point zero one and five moving on. We have the

chunks.title: Every_Python_Function_Explained___H_-_Z_online-video-cutter_WMi3hwO.com
chunks.text: print function. Now I'm sure many of you know how this works, but we can pass multiple arguments

chunks.title: Every_Python_Function_Explained___H_-_Z_online-video-cutter_WMi3hwO.com
chunks.text: here to the print function. They do not need to be strings and they will all be printed out,

chunks.title: Every_Python_Function_Explained___H_-_Z_online-video-cutter_WMi3hwO.com
chunks.text: separated by a space by default. Now, if you want to override the default separator, you can pass

chunks.title: Every_Python_Function_Explained___H_-_Z_online-video-cutter_WMi3hwO.com
chunks.text: this SEP keyword. So if I use a pipe now, hello, enrolled will be separated by a pipe. There will

chunks.title: Every_Python_Function_Explained___H_-_Z_online-video-cutter_WMi3hwO.com
chunks.text: not be a space. Alternatively, you can use this end keyword. You can also use it in combination

chunks.title: Every_Python_Function_Explained___H_-_Z_online-video-cutter_WMi3hwO.com
chunks.text: with SEP. And now this means at the end. So once this print statement is done, rather than printing

chunks.title: Every_Python_Function_Explained___H_-_Z_online-video-cutter_WMi3hwO.com
chunks.text: the default, which is an escape character, so backslash N, you're going to be printing this.

chunks.title: Every_Python_Function_Explained___H_-_Z_online-video-cutter_WMi3hwO.com
chunks.text: So usually what would happen is OK would be printed on the next line.

chunks.title: Every_Python_Function_Explained___H_-_Z_online-video-cutter_WMi3hwO.com
chunks.text: But since now we're not printing this backslash end, which forces the terminal to go to the next line,

chunks.title: Every_Python_Function_Explained___H_-_Z_online-video-cutter_WMi3hwO.com
chunks.text: you're going to see that this will be printed directly after hello.

chunks.title: Every_Python_Function_Explained___H_-_Z_online-video-cutter_WMi3hwO.com
chunks.text: So let's do this. And notice again, hello, world one, hello, pipe world, and then hello.

chunks.title: Every_Python_Function_Explained___H_-_Z_online-video-cutter_WMi3hwO.com
chunks.text: And and then OK, that's because, again, we didn't have this backslash end.

chunks.title: Every_Python_Function_Explained___H_-_Z_online-video-cutter_WMi3hwO.com
chunks.text: Now, if I manually add the backslash end in here and I print this out,

chunks.title: Every_Python_Function_Explained___H_-_Z_online-video-cutter_WMi3hwO.com
chunks.text: now you notice that we move down to the next line.

chunks.title: Every_Python_Function_Explained___H_-_Z_online-video-cutter_WMi3hwO.com
chunks.text: All right. So moving on, we have property.

chunks.title: Every_Python_Function_Explained___H_-_Z_online-video-cutter_WMi3hwO.com
chunks.text: And this allows us to protect an attribute by assigning a getter, setter and deleter.

chunks.title: Every_Python_Function_Explained___H_-_Z_online-video-cutter_WMi3hwO.com
chunks.text: You can see I have those here.

chunks.title: Every_Python_Function_Explained___H_-_Z_online-video-cutter_WMi3hwO.com
chunks.text: Then I have my property function and I pass my getter, setter and deleter.

chunks.title: Every_Python_Function_Explained___H_-_Z_online-video-cutter_WMi3hwO.com
chunks.text: You do not need to pass a delete function and you do not need to pass this optional.

chunks.title: Every_Python_Function_Explained___H_-_Z_online-video-cutter_WMi3hwO.com
chunks.text: What do you call this description of the property?

chunks.title: Every_Python_Function_Explained___H_-_Z_online-video-cutter_WMi3hwO.com
chunks.text: So now that I've done this, whenever I try to access X on my C class, it's going to use

chunks.title: Every_Python_Function_Explained___H_-_Z_online-video-cutter_WMi3hwO.com
chunks.text: the getter function to return the value to me.

chunks.title: Every_Python_Function_Explained___H_-_Z_online-video-cutter_WMi3hwO.com
chunks.text: When I try to set using an assignment like this, it's going to use the setter function

chunks.title: Every_Python_Function_Explained___H_-_Z_online-video-cutter_WMi3hwO.com
chunks.text: and pass my assigned value to value inside of this function.

chunks.title: Every_Python_Function_Explained___H_-_Z_online-video-cutter_WMi3hwO.com
chunks.text: If I tried to delete same thing, it would use my delete

chunks.title: Every_Python_Function_Explained___H_-_Z_online-video-cutter_WMi3hwO.com
chunks.text: method or my delete function.

chunks.title: Every_Python_Function_Explained___H_-_Z_online-video-cutter_WMi3hwO.com
chunks.text: All right. So that's that.

chunks.title: Every_Python_Function_Explained___H_-_Z_online-video-cutter_WMi3hwO.com
chunks.text: Now we can also use a newer syntax, which is the decorator syntax.

chunks.title: Every_Python_Function_Explained___H_-_Z_online-video-cutter_WMi3hwO.com
chunks.text: So I can decorate a function, which is the name of my property.

chunks.title: Every_Python_Function_Explained___H_-_Z_online-video-cutter_WMi3hwO.com
chunks.text: Then I can have a setter, deleter and getter.

chunks.title: Every_Python_Function_Explained___H_-_Z_online-video-cutter_WMi3hwO.com
chunks.text: And this works the exact same as just using the function plainly like this.

chunks.title: Every_Python_Function_Explained___H_-_Z_online-video-cutter_WMi3hwO.com
chunks.text: This is probably the preferred approach right now.

chunks.title: Every_Python_Function_Explained___H_-_Z_online-video-cutter_WMi3hwO.com
chunks.text: It's kind of modern Python,

chunks.title: Every_Python_Function_Explained___H_-_Z_online-video-cutter_WMi3hwO.com
chunks.text: but you can use this property as well.
